channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Says Vandals Sabotaged the Reflecting Pool. Internal Documents Raise Doubts.
item.description: The documents do not indicate that the peeling blue coating and algae blooms were caused intentionally.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and David A. Fahrenthold
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: The reflecting pool, seen from the Washington Monument, with workers attempting to contain the algae on Sunday.

item.title: N.S.A. Lost Access to Powerful A.I. Model Amid Anthropic Dispute
item.description: A recent episode underscored the Trump administration’s increasing reliance on advanced A.I. systems for cybersecurity even as it battles a leading U.S. developer.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz and Julian E. Barnes
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has labeled Anthropic, the maker of Mythos 5, a supply chain risk.

item.title: Photos Behind Kennedy Center Tarps Show No Sign of Trump’s Name
item.description: Images circulated by an activist group reveal bare marble where President Trump’s name once resided. The Kennedy Center previously told a federal judge it had been removed.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Hands Off the Arts
item.media:description: In a sworn declaration this month, the Kennedy Center said it had removed the words “The Donald J. Trump and” from its facade.

item.title: Protesters Accused of Antifa Ties Sentenced to Up to 100 Years in ICE Attack
item.description: The penalties, issued in an attack where a police officer was shot, dwarfed those given to Jan. 6 rioters and appeared to signal that at least some courts will deal aggressively with ICE protesters.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer and Krista Torralva
item.media:credit: Tony Gutierrez/Associated Press
item.media:description: The attack took place last year outside the Prairieland Detention Center in Alvarado, Texas.

item.title: New Yorkers Vote in High-Stakes Primaries
item.description: Also, the Strait of Hormuz sees a notable uptick in traffic. Here's the latest at the end of Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voters casting ballots in Brooklyn today.

item.title: Senate Votes to Direct End to Iran War, Rebuking Trump on War Powers
item.description: A few Republicans joined Democrats in favor of a measure instructing President Trump to halt military operations against Iran or seek congressional authorization.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Capitol seen on Tuesday. The vote came amid tensions over the war among the G.O.P., which faces a punishing political environment ahead of midterm elections.

item.title: Trump Is Making Big Claims About the Iran Talks. Iran Keeps Contradicting Him.
item.description: President Trump appears to be describing his preferences as fully negotiated deals, in hopes of locking the Iranians in. The question is whether a succession of such disputes will sink the whole venture.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: In the negotiation between the United States and Iran, leaks are replaced by official, if fragmentary, announcements — usually from the American side.

item.title: Tucker Carlson and Marjorie Taylor Greene Say They Are Done With the G.O.P.
item.description: Their split with the Republican Party represents an expansion of a feud with President Trump that could further complicate the party’s midterm prospects.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies
item.media:credit: Rebecca Noble for The New York Times; Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Tucker Carlson and Marjorie Taylor Greene.

item.title: As Chinese Tech Pulls Ahead, U.S. Fears It Will Become Dependent
item.description: Chinese firms have some of the world’s most advanced technology. But U.S. officials say relying on it could come with a downside.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Gilles Sabrié for The New York Times

item.title: China Takes Supercomputer Crown From U.S. For First Time Since 2017
item.description: A supercomputer in Shenzhen was declared the world’s fastest. It uses only standard microprocessors and not the special-purpose chips called graphics processing units.
item.dc:creator: Don Clark
item.media:credit: Xu Congjun/VCG, via Getty Images
item.media:description: China’s National Supercomputing Center in Wuxi in 2020. LineShine, a supercomputer in Shenzhen, has been declared the world’s fastest.

item.title: Searching for Clues in Jeffrey Epstein’s Boyhood
item.description: “Jeff” grew up in an insular world, kept company with a few brainy boys and fixated on girls. Was a monster hiding in plain sight?
item.dc:creator: Lisa Miller and Steve Eder

item.title: Supreme Court Bars Rastafarian Man From Suing Prison Guards for Shaving His Dreadlocks
item.description: Damon Landor, a Rastafarian, tried to sue Louisiana prison officials for violating his religious rights.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Damon Landor sought to sue state prison officials in Louisiana for forcibly shaving his head.

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Lawsuit Claiming Cisco Systems Helped China Target Falun Gong
item.description: The court’s decision could have broader implications for whether companies can be held liable for aiding in international human rights abuses.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: Falun Gong followers during a silent protest outside the Communist Party’s headquarters in Beijing in 1999.

item.title: Britain Is Still Deep in the Shadow of Brexit
item.description: Ten years after a slim majority voted to leave the European Union, the economic and political effects of that decision continue to disrupt the United Kingdom.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear and Megan Specia
item.media:credit: Adam Ferguson for The New York Times
item.media:description: A “Vote Leave” rally in London in June 2016. The proposal to separate from the European Union narrowly passed.

item.title: With Starmer’s Resignation, ‘Hot Podium Guy’ Has Outlasted Five U.K. Prime Ministers
item.description: A sound engineer has emerged as an unexpected heartthrob amid Britain’s political tumult.
item.dc:creator: Alisha Haridasani Gupta
item.media:credit: UK Pool, via Reuters

item.title: How Trump Turned America’s Refugee Program Into a Pathway for White People
item.description: President Trump has created an exception to his refugee ban for white South Africans, reshaping a program intended for people fleeing persecution and disaster.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Terry Ratzlaff for The New York Times
item.media:description: A white South African, Charl Kleinhaus secured refugee status and moved with his family to the United States in a matter of months.

item.title: A Look Inside the Welcome Bags Planned for White South African Refugees
item.description: The United States is putting together bags with a children’s book on so-called reverse racism, and with a document that defends the country’s founding on the basis of slavery.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:credit: Julia Demaree Nikhinson/Associated Press
item.media:description: Afrikaner refugees from South Africa arriving at Dulles International Airport in Virginia last May.

item.title: Meta Has Created a Prediction Markets App
item.description: The experimental app, internally called “Arena,” would be independent of Facebook and Instagram. It could compete for attention with Polymarket and Kalshi, the biggest prediction markets.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mark Zuckerberg, Meta’s chief executive, is making a broader push to create new types of apps based on emerging social behavior online.

item.title: U.S. Presses Meta to Agree to A.I. Reviews
item.description: Federal officials are urging the lone major tech company holdout to allow government safety evaluations, weeks after ordering Anthropic to pull its latest model.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle, Eli Tan and Sheera Frenkel
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Meta released its most recent artificial intelligence model in April.

item.title: Looking Back on the U.S.A.’s Many Founders, 250 Years In
item.description: In the past 50 years, the way we tell the story of the Revolution has become dramatically more complex. Can it still inspire us all?
item.dc:creator: Jane Kamensky
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Universal History Archive/Universal Images Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The five-man drafting committee presenting the Declaration of Independence to Congress, in an early 19th-century painting by John Trumbull.

item.title: If You Love America, Cringe for It
item.description: Let’s remember who we once were, because it’s what we may yet be again.
item.dc:creator: Bret Stephens
item.media:credit: Mandel Ngan/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: Trump’s Iran Debacle Could Be a Gift for America
item.description: There is at least a chance that America will not return to war with Iran, but find a way to turn the page on 47 years of animosity and confrontation.
item.dc:creator: Robert Malley and Stephen Wertheim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Illustration by Sam Whitney/The New York Times

item.title: Services Sold to Boost I.V.F. Odds Backed by Little Evidence, Study Finds
item.description: Some patients spend thousands of dollars on these extra ‘add-on’ procedures during fertility treatment.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Astor
item.media:credit: Claire Merchlinsky/The New York Times; Photographs via Getty
